text: This will be a discovery research interview and the questions will be open ended.
There are no right or wrong answers, so answer as honestly as you can.
Are you ready to get started?
Ready to start?
To start.
Could you walk me through the last time you browsed the Askable app and tell me what specifically caught your eye?
I browse the app and I looked for the new opportunities that have been uploaded.
Once you see those new opportunities, how do you decide which ones are actually worth clicking on?
I'll look at ones which might appeal to me.
Could you give me a specific example of an opportunity you saw recently that appealed to you?
One that appealed to my interests, maybe about sport or employment.
When you click on an opportunity like one about sports, what information are you looking for to decide if you'll actually apply?
Whether it's relevant for me and I would be eligible for it.
Tell me about a time you clicked on a study you thought was relevant, but then realized halfway through the application that you weren't actually eligible.
When I started doing this, it became clear that I wasn't eligible.
What specifically happened during that gameplay application that made you realize it wasn't a good match for you?
As I started to get through the screener, I became aware that due to the constraints, it wasn't eligible to me.
What specific constraint or question in that screener showed you that you weren't eligible?
Maybe it was asking for a certain age range or that you worked in a certain industry.
How did it feel to spend time on those screener questions only to find out about that industry requirement at the very end?
That was frustrating and it should have told me up front.
Thank you for sharing those insights.
Next, we're going to begin a website usability test.
I'll ask you to share your screen, provide you with some instructions for your task, and then open the website in your browser.
Please click the screen sharing button that appears and choose the option to share this window.
Perfect.
Here's your task.
Now I'm going to show you a prototype with new filtering features.
As you explore, please think out loud.
Tell me everything you notice, what you're thinking and any questions that come to mind.
Imagine you just opened the app during your lunch break with 15 minutes free on your phone.
Explore the prototype to find opportunities that would work for that situation.
Remember, we're testing the design, not you, so any confusion or friction you experience is valuable feedback.
Please think aloud as you work through this, sharing your thoughts and describing what you're seeing as you go.
Let me know when you feel like you've completed the task.
I'm going to open the website in your browser now.
If you don't see the website, click the button below to open it.
Go ahead and describe what you're seeing as you explore the prototype.
So this looks great.
We've got the the Askable app.
So they've got the short tasks and surveys up at the top and then we've got sessions to schedule, so video calls, in person, diary studies, interviews.
So that looks really great.
There's a lot of information there.
I can see some new little bubbles, so they're telling me that it's new and also ones which are differentiating between ones like a diary study and AI interview.
And they're telling me the date and the time and the incentive amount.
You mentioned seeing some new little bubbles and different labels for the studies.
As you're looking at those details, what's going through your mind regarding finding something that fits into that 15 minute lunch break?
Yeah, there's more information and it makes it easier to know whether it's applicable to me, whether it's new, how long it takes, the device that it's relevant for.
I noticed you mentioned it makes it easier to know if it's applicable.
What specific information on the screen is helping you determine how long it takes?
Well, it tells me the time, duration and the dates, whether it's in person or online, and the type of activity.
As you look at those activities, what stands out to you as something that would fit into that 15 minute window?
You have.
Probably a little AI interview for 10 minutes there about the mobile or Internet.
What makes that 10 minute AI interview about mobile or Internet more appealing for your lunch break than the other options you're seeing?
Well, it only goes for 10 minutes, so that would fit into my lunch break.
As you're browsing these options, what else are you noticing on the screen that might help you narrow down your choices quickly?
Whether it's an interview or a survey.
What specifically about the way those are displayed helps you distinguish between them?
The label up at the top which says AI interview or survey.
As you explore those labels, feel free to try out any of the features you see on the screen.
What are you noticing as you interact with the page?
Yeah, I can see all of the different icons and there are different colors as well, so that's great.
What do those different colors signify to you as you're looking at them?
The red signifies AI interview.
The yellow is for diary study, pink for video call, white for in person.
As you're looking at those different colored labels, what's your impression of how that information is organized?
Yeah.
Makes it easy to categorize and makes it easy to understand.
As you keep exploring the prototype, what else are you finding that helps you decide which opportunities to click on.
All of the little bubbles, like whether it's for mobile or mobile and desktop or either whether it's new and the incentive amount.
What does seeing that information about the device like mobile or desktop mean for you in this lunch break scenario?
Well, the mobile would be suitable because I don't have a desktop available to me at the moment because I'm on my lunch break.
As you look at the options specifically for mobile, what are you seeing that lets you know if there's anything else you can do to sort through these choices?
Well, it tells me that, yeah, I've got mobile and I can probably filter some of the results if I want to choose.
If I want to choose mobile only, so I can apply the filter for mobile only and then that will bring them up for me.
Go ahead and try that out if you'd like.
What are you noticing as you interact with those filtering options?
So I've filtered down, so it's mobile only, so these are ones that you don't need a desktop for.
As you look at those filtered results for mobile, how does this view compare to the one you saw before?
Yes, so it's been personalized down to the filter that I chose, so that's excellent.
What specifically do you see on the screen now that lets you know the filter has been applied?
It says mobile only and it says that it's showing the nine opportunities that are mobile only.
As you look at those nine opportunities, what's catching your eye now?
Well, they're all applicable for me because I've got a mobile, so now I just have to choose one which would be good for my lunch break.
So probably just a short task or surveys.
What makes you lean towards a short task right now?
Because I'm only on a lunch break, so I don't have time for a longer task.
As you look at the options for short tasks, what are you seeing that helps you decide which one to go for?
Maybe a survey would probably be a little bit quicker than an AI interview, so that might be good.
If I don't have a lot of time.
What are you seeing on the screen that indicates how much time that survey might take compared to the other options?
Well, they both say 10 minutes, but in my experience I've seen that the surveys can generally be a bit quicker.
As you're looking at these options that are Both labeled as 10 minutes.
Is there anything else on the screen you're curious about or would want to try?
No, I don't think so.
It sounds like you've explored the features that were relevant to your lunch break.
Shall we move on from this prototype?
Yes.
Perfect.
Thank you.
Please close the website task and we'll continue our conversation.
Okay, let's continue.
Thank you so much for your time and for sharing such detailed feedback today.
Your insights on how you prioritize mobile tasks during your lunch break will be incredibly helpful for our team.
